coredata.dc:title: An electrochemical strategy for synthesising carbon-based nanomaterials with tuned redox properties
coredata.dc:creator: Veloso, A.D.
Videira, R.A.
Oliveira, M.C.
coredata.dc:description: Previous work with electrogenerated hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials holds enormous promise in cell redox-modulation nanomedicine when its electrochemical production is run in a citric acid buffer. To further understand the role of tri-/di-carboxylic acid buffers on the nanomaterials redox properties, six different carboxylic acids buffers with biological relevance were used in their electrochemical preparation, followed by a fully cyclic voltammetric characterization of the resulting anolytes. The results show that the carbon-based nanomaterial may exhibit electron-donating, electron-accepting properties, or any electroactivity. Very strong electron-donating properties are found in nanomaterials generated in carboxylic acids with α-C(OH) groups (citric, malic, and tartaric acids). It is demonstrated that the electron-donating nanomaterials are in a stable semiquinone-like form, whose concentration enhances in a time-dependent process after the terminus of the electrosynthesis in citric and malic acid buffers. A comprehensive interpretation of the role of the carboxylic acid during the electrogeneration of the nanomaterials, and under no external driving force, is provided. The overall study opens a new way to tune the redox activity of carbon-based nanomaterials and provides new perspectives for redox cycling in the biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mechanism
Electron shuttle
Radical stability
Graphite
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of electrogenerated hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials
Screening of the intrinsic electrochemical activity of EHC nanomaterials
Electrochemical characterization of electron-accepting ehc nanomaterial
Electrochemical characterization of electron-donating ehc nanomaterials
Stability of EHC@citric, tartaric, and malic solutions
Buffer concentration effect on the EHC@citric formation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Portugal's Foundation for Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Laboratório Associado para a Química Verde
Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia
Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank FCT (Portugal's Foundation for Science and Technology) for financial support through the CQ-VR (UIDB/00616/2020, UIDP/00616/2020) and LAQV/REQUIMTE (UIDB/50006/2020) Research Units. Andreia D. Veloso thanks FCT, MCTES, ESF, and EU through the individual research pH. D for the Ph.D. scholarship (SFRH/BD/138425/2018).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammograms of a glassy carbon electrode in EHC solutions prepared using different carboxylic acid buffers (black lines) and the respective blank solutions (dashed line). Arrows indicate the direction of the scans from the open circuit potential; ν= 50 mV s −1.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of EHC@maleic solution at different scan rates. (b) Plot of the peaks current density vs the square root of the scan rate. (c) Cyclic voltammograms of blank solution at different scan rates. (d) Plot of current density at −0.075 V vs the scan rate in the EHC and the blank solutions.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms at ν= 50 mV s − 1 of EHC@maleic solution after different pH adjustments; (b) Plot of ∆Ep (black diamonds) and midpoint redox potential (red squares) vs pH.
(a-c) Cyclic voltammograms of the EHC solutions at different scan rates. (a’-c’) Plots of the oxidation peak current vs the square root of the scan rate.
(a-c) Cyclic voltammograms of EHC solutions after pH adjustment; v = 50 mV s−1. (a’-c’) Plot of Ep vs pH.

Cyclic voltammograms obtained by extending the cathodic limit to more negative potentials in different EHC (full line) and blank (dashed line) solutions; Inset: a more negative cathodic limit potential was used. ν= 50 mV s −1.

Cyclic voltammograms of EHC@citric (a, b, c), EHC@malic (d, e, f), and EHC@tartaric (g, h, i) solutions after 4- and 15- days of storage at T = 25, 4, and −18 ºC. It is also included the voltammograms of as-prepared solutions, which were recorded approximately 1 hour after the switch-off of the galvanostatic assay; ν= 50 mV s − 1. The voltammograms were recorded at screen-print carbon electrodes in the air atmosphere.

Plot of the ratio j p/j p0 vs the duration of storage of EHC@citric (a), EHC@malic (b), and EHC@tartaric (c) solutions. jp0 represents the current intensity of the oxidation peak immediately after the EHC solution transfer from the synthesis-cell to the electrochemical characterization-cell. j p is the current density recorded at different times after the terminus of the electrochemical EHC generation.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of as-prepared EHC@citric solutions generated using different citric acid buffer concentrations; (b) Plot of the ratio j p/j p0 vs storage duration of EHC@citric solutions, which were generated using different buffer concentrations. j p0 is the oxidation current density recorded immediately after the transfer of the solution from the synthesis cell to the electrochemical characterization-cell and j p is the current density recorded at different times after the terminus of the electrochemical EHC generation.

Mechanistic proposal for the chemical reactions that occur in the anodic compartment during the graphite electrodes polarization under the galvanostatic mode, and after ending the galvanostatic assay, in electrolytic solutions containing (a) citric buffer and (b) tartaric buffer solutions.
Structures of carboxylic acids used in this study.

Electrochemical parameters obtained from the cyclic voltammograms of as-prepared EHC solutions at ν= 50 mV s − 1 on a glassy carbon electrode. Data correspond to the mean value of 4 independent freshly synthesized EHC solutions.

head.ce:title: An electrochemical strategy for synthesising carbon-based nanomaterials with tuned redox properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CQ-VR and Departamento de Química, Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro, Vila Real, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCQ-VR and Departamento de Química, Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro, Vila Real, Portugal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: REQUIMTE/LAQV, Laboratório de Farmacognosia, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Farmácia, Universidade do Porto, Porto, Portugal
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bREQUIMTE/LAQV, Laboratório de Farmacognosia, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Farmácia, Universidade do Porto, Porto, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Previous work with electrogenerated hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials holds enormous promise in cell redox-modulation nanomedicine when its electrochemical production is run in a citric acid buffer. To further understand the role of tri-/di-carboxylic acid buffers on the nanomaterials redox properties, six different carboxylic acids buffers with biological relevance were used in their electrochemical preparation, followed by a fully cyclic voltammetric characterization of the resulting anolytes. The results show that the carbon-based nanomaterial may exhibit electron-donating, electron-accepting properties, or any electroactivity. Very strong electron-donating properties are found in nanomaterials generated in carboxylic acids with α-C(OH) groups (citric, malic, and tartaric acids). It is demonstrated that the electron-donating nanomaterials are in a stable semiquinone-like form, whose concentration enhances in a time-dependent process after the terminus of the electrosynthesis in citric and malic acid buffers. A comprehensive interpretation of the role of the carboxylic acid during the electrogeneration of the nanomaterials, and under no external driving force, is provided. The overall study opens a new way to tune the redox activity of carbon-based nanomaterials and provides new perspectives for redox cycling in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of carbon-based nanomaterials with properties that enable their use in therapeutic applications is a notably challenging task in nanomedicine. These nanomaterials should be nontoxic, biocompatible, and exhibit colloidal stability [1–7]. However, the spectrum of therapeutic applications of nanomaterials is mainly dependent on other properties, including the presence of suitable functional groups to allow their functionalization with specific biomolecules (e.g., peptides, nucleic acids, lipids), ability to preserve their optical properties in the biological environment, competence to overcome the physiological barriers and reach the therapeutic target, and a redox behavior compatible with the functional organization of biological systems [8–10].

To provide the potential use of carbon-based nanomaterials as redox mediators/modulators in therapeutical applications, these should fulfill various requisites: a) ability to change the oxidation state in a reversible or chemically regenerative way; b) ability to react in a fast electron transfer process with electron-donors and/or electron-acceptors involved in cell redox balance; c) possess high water solubility and capacity to diffuse in the intracellular aqueous medium. Most of these requirements may be assessed electrochemically.

The term carbon-based nanomaterials comprise a wide range of carbon nanomaterials from graphene oxide (GO) to the smaller ones, like carbon dots (CDs) [11] and hydrophilic carbon clusters (HCC) [12]. Most of the carbon-based nanomaterials that display an intrinsic electrochemical behavior possess oxidizing properties (e.g., GO, rGO, HCC), however, therapeutic applications of these types of nanomaterials are restricted by their low water-solubility, reduced bioavailability, cytotoxicity, and/or by the high potential required to reduce them (typically E < −0.90 V vs Ag/AgCl, pH 7.4) [13–15], sometimes outside the range of redox potential where animal cells operate.

In general, CDs do not have water solubility and toxicity constraints, but most of them do not show any electroactivity [16,17]. Even though some works report that CDs can show an inherent electroactivity, their electrochemical behavior is very difficult to predict, being highly dependent on the carbon source and oxidations conditions used in their preparation. For example, CDs synthesized from graphene display an irreversible oxidation peak (Eoxid. = 0.90 V vs Ag/AgCl, pH 7.2) [18] whereas a reversible redox couple was observed on CDs obtained from the combustion soot of natural gas (E0´ = −0.05 V vs Ag/AgCl, pH 5) [19], and an irreversible reduction peak was depicted with CDs prepared from single-walled carbon nanotubes (Ered. = -0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl, pH 7.4) [13].

In contrast, the electrochemical behavior of the newly developed carbonaceous material, named Electrogenerated Hydrophilic Carbon (EHC), is highly reproducible [20]. This type of nanomaterial, produced from a graphite anode under strong polarization conditions, displays outstanding solubility in water and a structure dominated by aromatic sp2 carbon atoms in a non-ordered carbon network. Most of the carbon particles are < 2 nm in diameter, close or beyond the resolution of the AFM microscope. Recently, it was demonstrated that whenever the EHC material is generated in a citrate buffer it shows strong electron-donor properties and a very good ability to protect cells against oxidative damage and toxicity promoted by external toxic agents [21].

These findings have addressed our attention to the potential applications of this new class of carbon nanomaterials in the therapeutic field, and the prospective ability to tune their redox properties by the type of carboxylic acid used as the electrolyte in the synthesis process.

In this context, the present work has two major purposes: to contribute to the understanding of the structural effect of the carboxylic acid on the mechanism responsible for the redox properties of the EHC nanomaterial and to gain fundamental insight into the electron transfer mechanism responsible for their electroactivity. Issues of utmost importance for the rational formulation design of any emerging therapeutic group of nanomaterials. In addition, it will be also evaluated the temporal stability of the as-prepared nanomaterials at different storage conditions. With these objectives in mind, the EHC nanomaterials were generated in this work using six different buffers prepared with carboxylic acids that have biological relevance (citric, malic, maleic, malonic, succinic, and tartaric) [22,23]. These are tri- and di-carboxylic acids that allow working in similar pH conditions and can be clustered according to their molecular structures. After their preparation, a detailed mechanistic study was immediately undertaken with each EHC solution.

Citric acid, disodium salt sesquihydrate (C6H6Na2O7, 99%), DL- tartaric acid (C4H6O6, 99.5%), and succinic acid (C4H6O4, 99.0%) were acquired from ACROS Organics™, l-(-) -Malic acid disodium salt (C4H4Na2O5, 95–100%), l-(-) -malic acid disodium salt (C4H5NaO5, 95–10 0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Malonic acid (C3H2O4, > 99%), maleic acid (C4H4O4, > 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%), and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 96%) were obtained from Merck. Trisodium citrate 2-hydrate (C6H5Na3O7. 2H2O, 99.0%) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2 30% (W/V) were bought from Panreac. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was acquired from Fisher BioReagents. Water dispersions of graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQD) were purchased from Methrom Dropsens. Test strips of hydrogen peroxide were obtianed from Macherey-Nagel (Quantofix). All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used to prepare all buffer solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrogenerated hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: EHC nanomaterials were prepared according to the procedure that was previously described [20,21,24]. Succinctly, it was performed a galvanostatic polarization (I = 60 mA, t = 1 h) in a three-compartment cell containing graphite rods (99.997%, Goodfellow) and a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode. The electrodes were polarized by an Autolab PGSTAT100 potentiostat/galvanostat. The electrolysis was performed under an argon atmosphere keeping the cell inside of a dark Faraday cage. More details about the nanomaterial's preparation can be found in Supporting Information (Sections 1.1 and 1.2).

As mentioned before, six different carboxylic acid buffers were used as the electrolyte. For the sake of simplification, the name of the buffers refers to the carboxylic acid: citric, tartaric, malic, maleic, malonic, and succinic, Table 1
. Hence, the following designation was adopted for the different as-prepared EHC solutions: EHC@carboxylic acid. All of them were prepared with a pH close to the respective pKa2 or pKa3 and similar ionic strength.

After one hour of electrode polarization, the solutions containing the EHC nanomaterial were transferred from the anodic compartment of the synthesis cell to the electrochemical characterization cell. For comparatives purposes, the electrochemical characterization was also performed with a blank solution, which contains the corresponding buffer electrolyte. The pH of the blank solution was adjusted with 2 M H2SO4 to the final pH of the EHC solution.

The electrochemical characterization cell is a one-compartment amber electrochemical cell (volume ≅ 15 ml) containing a glassy carbon working electrode (ALS Co.), a Pt counter-electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (NaCl 3 M) micro-reference electrode (ALS Co.). The solution was deaerated for 30 min before the electrochemical characterization took place. An argon atmosphere was kept over the solution during all the electrochemical characterization processes. Before each assay, the electrode surface was polished with an alumina slurry (0.3 µm), rinsed with ultrapure water, and ultrasonicated by turns in water for 1 min, for a total of 3 min. All potentials are reported vs. Ag/AgCl (NaCl 3 M).

For the study of the pH effect, the pH value was brought to the desired value by adding 4 M NaOH or 2 M H2SO4 solutions. Note that the pH dependence was analyzed and compared in solutions submitted to successive increments (or decrements) of pH, followed by successive pH adjustments in the opposite direction. Comparable voltammograms were obtained.

The effect of the storage temperature in the EHC stability was evaluated using carbon screen-printed electrodes (ref. DRP-110, DropSens, Spain) and EHC solution that was previously stored at a set temperature (25, 4, and −18 ºC) for a certain period (4 and 15 days) and then allowed to reach room temperature before the voltammetric assays were carried out. The voltammograms were recorded under an air atmosphere. The potentials are reported versus a pseudo-reference Ag electrode.

TEM images of dialyzed EHC@malonic and EHC@succinic solutions were acquired at 100 kV with Jeol JEM 1010. Electron paramagnetic spectroscopy (EPR) spectra of EHC@citric and tartaric were obtained at 120 K in a Bruker EMX ER073 spectrometer (X-Band) equipped with a standard 4102ST TE102 rectangular cavity (Bruker Corporation, Billerica, MA). The microwave frequency was 9.448 GHz with a power of 1.006 mW. The spectra were acquired with a sweep width of 6000 G and a measurement of 2048 complex points. After Fourier Transformation the spectrum baseline was corrected by visual identification of several noise points along the full baseline. The spectrum of EHC@citric was measured with 20 scans with the external magnetic field centered at 3262 gauss. The spectrum of EHC@tartaric was measured with 10 scans with the external magnetic field centered at 3024 gauss.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of the intrinsic electrochemical activity of EHC nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate and compare the ability of the as-prepared EHC nanomaterials in solution to donate and/or receive electrons within the range of potentials with biological relevance, the electrode potential was scanned in the anodic and cathodic direction from the open circuit potential, respectively, in a short-range potential (approximately 0.5 V). The voltammograms of the first positive-going and negative-going reverse scans and corresponding electrochemical data are presented in Fig. 1
and Table 2
, respectively. One of the following behaviors can be depicted: the EHC shows a high ability for electron-donating (EHC@citric, malic, and tartaric) or electron-accepting (EHC@maleic), or no electroactivity is shown (EHC@succinic and malonic).

To confirm that the observed voltammetric behavior was exclusively assigned to the EHC nanomaterials generated by the graphite anode, rather than by any oxidation/reduction product of the carboxylic acids, control experiments were performed by replacing the graphite anode or/and cathode by a platinum electrode in the different electrolytic solutions. This procedure was then followed by the voltammetric analysis of the solution removed from the anodic compartment. No voltammetric peaks were disclosed whenever the graphite anode was replaced by a platinum electrode. In contrast, by substituting the cathode but keeping the anode of graphite, the voltammetric characteristics depicted in Fig. 1 prevailed, which confirms that the observed voltammetric behavior is strictly assigned to the carbon-based nanomaterials generated by the graphite anode.

For comparison purposes, the voltammograms of phosphate-buffered saline solutions (PBS) containing commercial carbon-based nanomaterials (GO solution and Graphene Quantum Dots, both supplied by Metrohm Dropsens) were also recorded and compared to EHC@malic, citric and tartaric solutions after their pH adjustment to the neutral pH (Supporting Information- Section 2.1). No oxidations peaks were depicted unless a rather high overpotential was applied (solely in the GO solution), reinforcing the singular behavior displayed by the nanomaterials contained in EHC@malic, tartaric, and citric solutions.

Although no voltammetric peaks were detected in the malonic and succinic EHC solutions, a massive amount of carbon nanoparticles was electrochemically generated (Supporting Information – Section 2.2). The apparent non-electroactivity of these carbon-based nanomaterials can emerge from the adsorption of organic compounds (the electrolyte buffer itself or electrolyte oxidation products) on the surface and edges of the EHC nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of electron-accepting ehc nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para:
EHC@maleic shows one cathodic peak, peak 1a, at E c = −0.425 V in the forward scanning direction, and one anodic peak, peak 1b, at E a= −0.266 V, in the reverse scan, at 50 mV s − 1 ( Table 2 ). Even though the ratio of j a/j c is one, and the midpoint redox potential (E a+ E c)/2 = −0.345 ± 0.034 V) is independent of the scan rate, the peak potential separation of this pair of redox peaks is very large and scan rate dependent, with ΔE p = 135–192 mV (from 10 to 200 mV s − 1), typical of a slow heterogeneous electron reaction, Fig. 2
a. Based on | Ep -E p/2 | ≅ 68 ± 11 mV, αn a= 0.72 ± 0.11 was obtained (α represents the electron transfer coefficient and n a is the number of electrons transferred in the rate-determining step), pointing for a two-electron reaction.

Fig. 2 b shows a linear relationship of j p vs the square root of the scan rate, consistent with a diffusion-limited electrode reaction. The scan rate effect also reveals a high double-layer current in comparison with the blank solution, Fig. 2 c. The capacitance of the electrode in EHC@maleic solution, calculated from the current density at −0.075 V vs scan rate, shows that it is approximately three times larger than in the blank solution, Fig. 2 d. This behavior is indicative that a carbon-based nanomaterial with non-electroactivity but the ability to adsorb on the electrode surface was also generated. It is probably this surface species that is responsible for the slow heterogeneous electron transfer kinetics displayed by the electroactive EHC nanomaterial in the EHC@maleic solution.

The effect of pH on the redox peaks pair was also investigated, Fig. 3
a. Unexpectedly, the potential peak separation (ΔE p) was found to be pH-dependent, suggesting that the electrode kinetics is substantially faster in the alkaline medium than in the acid medium. A possible explanation for this atypical behavior relies on the coverage decrease of non-electroactive carbon-based material on the electrode surface as the pH of the solution increases (electrostatic repulsion effect), resulting in a more conductive electrode material, and consequently a faster heterogeneous electron transfer rate.

The midpoint potential of this redox couple was found to converge, within the alkaline range, to the formal potential of the redox couple detected in the hydrophilic nanomaterial generated in the inorganic phosphate buffer solution (EHC@phosphate) [25]. The pH dependence of this midpoint potential ( Fig. 3 b) was also consistent with an electrode reaction involving the same number of electrons and protons, similarly to what was reported in EHC@phosphate, pointing out the formation of quinone-like moieties [25].

Extending to a more negative limit the potential window in the negative-going sweep, a well-defined irreversible reduction peak at −0.78 V, peak 1c, is disclosed (Supporting information – Section 2.3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of electron-donating ehc nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Based on |Ep-Ep/2|, the αn a values calculated for the EHC@citric, malic, and tartaric solutions were 0.48 ± 0.06, 0.50 ± 0.03, 0.56 ± 0.05, respectively. Admitting the ideal electron transfer coefficient (α=0.5), the rate-determining step involves one electron (n a=1).

Successive scans without polishing the electrode between the cycles demonstrate that a steady current is reached. At 50 mV s − 1, the steady current achieves 30–40% of the current recorded in the first scan, but at 10 mV s − 1 its intensity is very close to the current peak recorded in the first scan (Supporting Information – Section 2.4). Several conclusions can be deduced from this behavior: a) the EHC nanomaterials are rather stable in solution within the time scale of the voltammetric experiments; b) the oxidation product(s) do not hinder the electrode reaction; c) the diffusibility of the different EHC nanomaterials in aqueous solution is very similar.

Fig. 4
shows the effect of the scan rate on the voltammetric behavior of the electron-donating EHC nanomaterials. A linear correlation between the oxidation peak intensity and the square root of the scan rate is observed, indicative that the electrode reaction is controlled by the diffusion of the EHC nanomaterials in the aqueous solution. On account of the diffusion similarities of the various EHC nanomaterials, the slopes of the plots j p vs ν 1/2 must reflect the fairly different concentration of the electroactive nanomaterial in the three buffer solutions.

The effect of pH on the voltammetric behavior of EHC@citric, malic, and tartaric is shown in Fig. 5
. The E p shifts linearly to less positive values as the pH increases until it becomes pH-independent. Taking into consideration the slope of 120 mV/pH unit and the predicted number of electrons, it can be concluded that the oxidation of EHC@citric involves the transfer of two protons and one electron at pH < 4.5. For EHC@tartaric and EHC@malic, the slope is indicative that the number of protons transferred is close to the number of electrons (probably one-electron and one-proton at pH < 4.2 and 5.9, respectively). For pH > 9, the voltammetric profile of these EHC materials changes dramatically, suggesting that EHC is not stable at such medium giving rise to new electrochemically active species (Supporting Information – Section 2.5).

Despite the intersection that results from the two straight lines drawn on the plots of Ep vs pH is close to the pK a of the carboxylic acid (pK a2citric acid = 4.80, pK a2malic acid = 5.13, pKa2tartaric acid = 4.37), it is not expected that this breaking point is caused by the presence of the carboxylic groups since it was shown that these groups (or any product of their oxidation) are electrochemically inactive on the glassy carbon electrode. On the other hand, Fig. 5 is also indicative that at physiological pH these EHC nanomaterials are redox-active without affecting the buffering capacity of biological systems.

Interestingly, Fig. 5 also shows that the peak current dependence on pH follows a different trend in EHC@citric, compared to EHC@tartaric and EHC@malic solutions. Such difference is probably the result of distinctive functionalization patterns on the three EHC surfaces, and consequently on their hydrophilic/hydrophobic character. A comparative structural characterization of the three different EHC nanomaterials will be addressed in a shortcoming publication for a better rationalization of the different electrochemical behaviors.

To further characterize the electron-donating properties of the as-prepared EHC nanomaterials, the potential window was extended to 0.90 V in the first-going positive scan. No other peaks were observed, except in the EHC@citric solution which exhibits an oxidation peak at 0.586 V (peak 3a in Supporting Information – Section 2.6), which may be associated with the oxidation of OH functional groups bonded to aromatic rings [29].

Extending the potential window in the negative-going sweep, other voltammetric features are disclosed in the EHC solutions, Fig. 6
, as well in the blank solutions (Supporting information – Section 2.7). EHC@citric shows two broad and weak cathodic peaks, peak 2c, at ≅ −0.80, and peak 3c, at ≅ −1.10 V. EHC@malic shows one cathodic, peak 4c, at −0.64 V. According to the literature, the peak at ≅ −0.64 V may be assigned to peroxyl [15], the peak at ≅ −0.80 V may be attributed to epoxide [30] (also observed in EHC@ maleic), whereas the peak at ≅ −1.10 V may be assigned to aldehyde groups [15]. Surprisingly, no cathodic peaks were observed in the EHC@tartaric, Fig. 6 c, indicative that no reducible oxygen-containing functional groups (epoxides, aldehydes, and peroxides) exist on that EHC nanomaterial.

It is intriguing the abrupt decrease in the reduction current that is observed in EHC@malic and EHC@tartaric solutions (inset Fig. 6) from approximately −1.0 V. Although a current decay is also observed in EHC@citric solution, it seems to occur to a much lesser extent. This behavior led us to hypothesize that other species, in addition to the EHC nanomaterial, may be formed during the galvanostatic assay or immediately after its ending. Since the voltammetric profile resembles the results previously obtained with an H2O2 containing solution on an EHC/GC modified electrode [31], two different experiments were performed to evaluate whether the current decay may result from the presence of hydrogen peroxide in the as-prepared EHC solutions. First, semi-quantitative analysis was performed using test strips for hydrogen peroxide (Supporting Information – Section 2.8). The results reveal the presence of H2O2 and point out its concentration dependence during the post-electrolysis period. In EHC@citric and EHC@malic solutions, the initial H2O2 concentration is, apparently, zero, but an increase in its concentration is observed over the next 5 h. In contrast, in the EHC@tartaric solution, the initial H2O2 concentration is rather high, but after 1.5 h it starts to decrease. Besides, cyclic voltammograms were recorded in H2O2 solutions containing the corresponding buffer electrolyte at the pH of the as-prepared EHC solution. The obtained voltammetric profiles are consistent with the hypothesis that the observed current decay in EHC solutions is attributed to the presence of H2O2 (Supporting Information – Section 2.8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of EHC@citric, tartaric, and malic solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Like any other nanomaterial with potential for biomedical applications, it is crucial to characterize the time-dependent stability of these EHC nanomaterials at different storage conditions. Typically, the nanomaterials' stability is appraised in terms of their tendency to form aggregates. In this work, stability evaluation will be particularly focused on the time-dependent redox activity of EHC nanomaterials. For this purpose, two different experiments were performed: (1) the electrochemical behavior of the as-prepared EHC solutions was investigated after their storage for a long period (up to 15 days) at three different temperatures (25, 4, and −18 ºC); (2) the cyclic voltammograms of the as-prepared EHC solutions were recorded over a short post-synthesis period (six hours), maintaining the EHC solutions at room temperature under an argon stream.

For studying the temperature and time storage effects on the voltammetric behavior of EHC solutions (experiment 1), three aliquots of approximately 1 mL were removed immediately after the generation of EHC nanomaterial and stored under an argon atmosphere at 25, 4, and −18 ºC. Due to the small volume available, the voltammetric analysis was performed on carbon screen-printed electrodes (SPE), under air atmosphere, and at room temperature. The results are shown in Fig. 7
. EHC@tartaric solution exhibits the lowest chemical stability as demonstrated by the current peak decrease after 4 and 15 days of storing at all temperature conditions. After 15 days its electron-donating ability is no longer retained unless the EHC solution is stored at low temperatures (−18 ºC); it is worth noting that storing the EHC@tartaric solution at −18 ºC for 15 days leads to a significant shift of the potential peak towards less positive potentials, whereas a 25% decrease is detected in the current response.

A completely different trend is observed in EHC@citric and EHC@malic solutions. Regarding the EHC@citric solution, a 35% current peak increase is unexpectedly detected after 4-days of storage at room temperature. At 4 ºC and −18 ºC, a current increase is also observed but its magnitude is smaller. After 15-days of storage, an increase in the oxidation current is still depicted at −18 ºC, whereas a current decay is detected if the solution is stored at 25 ºC or 4 ºC. Regarding the EHC@malic solution, a significant current decrease is observed in the first 4 days of storage at room temperature, but afterward a current increase is detected, even after 10 months (Supporting Information – Section 2.9). This behavior contrasts with what is observed when the solution is stored at 4 ºC or −18 ºC since no current increase is detected in the first 15 days.

To analyze the stability of the EHC solutions over a post-synthesis shorter period (6 h), the cyclic voltammograms were recorded on a glassy carbon working electrode keeping the solution in the electrochemical cell under an argon stream, at room temperature. The time-dependent effects on the current response are illustrated in Fig. 8
. At 360 min (6 h) post-synthesis, a current decrease of approximately 30% and 20% is detected in the EHC@tartaric and EHC@malic solutions, respectively, whereas a 30% increase is recorded in the EHC@citric solution. This data is consistent with the results shown above for the 4-days storage of the EHC solutions at room temperature. In summary, if the EHC solutions are stored at room temperature the concentration of EHC@citric increases in a short or long post-electrolysis period (e.g. 1 h to 4 days), the concentration of EHC@malic decreases in the same short period followed by a progressive increase, and the concentration of EHC@tartaric decreases independently of the post-electrolysis duration. Altogether, the results reveal three different balances between the rate of the reductant-like EHC nanomaterial degradation/consumption in the bulk solution and the rate of its formation under no external driving force. These different balances are not so evident if the solutions are stored at low temperatures (4 ºC and −18 ºC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Buffer concentration effect on the EHC@citric formation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether the buffer electrolyte was responsible for the observed increase of the electron-donator EHC species during the post-synthesis period, the effect of the buffer concentration used in the EHC synthesis on the peak current density was investigated. For that purpose, it was selected the citrate buffer. More details about the buffer composition and the galvanostatic assays can be found in Supporting Information – Section 2.10.

Fig. 9
a shows the cyclic voltammograms of as-prepared EHC@citric nanomaterials solutions generated using citric acid buffer concentrations within the 0.044–0.178 M range. It is demonstrated that the voltammetric behavior of the samples recorded immediately after the nanomaterials generation (included the time for the solution transfer from the synthesis cell to the voltammetric cell) is independent of the citrate buffer concentration. However, the evolution of j p/j p0 ratio along the time reveals that as the resting time of the EHC solution increases, the current density becomes strongly dependent on the buffer concentration, Fig. 9 b (corresponding voltammograms in Supporting Information – Section 2.10). These results indicate that the electrolyte itself participates in the reaction that is responsible for the formation of the carbon-based material that possesses electron-donating properties. Preliminary results point to a second-order rate of the EHC@citric oxidation reaction regarding the buffer electrolyte (Supporting Information – Section 2.10).

Since EHC nanomaterials are known to possess a high O/C ratio [21,32], it is thus expected that they exhibit electron-accepting properties, being their reduction potential conditioned by the oxygen functional group's moieties. Accordingly, apart from EHC@tartaric, at least one cathodic peak was detected in the first negative-going sweep in the EHC solutions.

In contrast, electron-donating properties are unforeseen with nanomaterials generated in the anodic compartment. To address this apparent paradox, we considered the possibility that the EHC nanomaterial is present in the form of a free radical whenever it is produced in the presence of carboxylic acids/carboxylates salts with α-C(OH) groups. To evaluate this hypothesis, the as-prepared EHC@citric, tartaric and malic solutions were analyzed by Electron Paramagnetic Spin (EPR). Preliminary results (Supporting Information – Section 3.1) confirmed unequivocally the presence of unpaired electrons in EHC@citric and EHC@tartaric solutions. In the EHC@malic solution, the EPR sign was very low (data not shown), probably due to the low nanomaterials content.

Further evidence that the nanomaterial generated in the presence of citric, tartaric, and malic buffers exhibit a free radical-like behavior is also given by the pKa assessed from Fig. 5 b. Accordingly, a pK a of 4–6 is consistent with semiquinone-like species [33,34]. The number of electrons transferred in the electrooxidation reaction estimated from the electrochemical data (n = 1, assuming α=0.5) also corroborates this hypothesis. Thus, it is predicted that all the three electron-donating nanomaterials are in a stable semiquinone-like form (represented here by the EHCSQ • formula), despite their putative structural particularities. Indeed, the slight differences found in their electron-donating abilities (reflected in the E onset and |E-E1
/2|) and acidity strengths (reflected in pK a´s) provide evidence that small differences in the electron density of their aromatic core must exist. These differences may be the result of diverse substituents in the honeycomb lattice of carbon (electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups) or differences in the chemical solution environment which may end in distinct intermolecular hydrogen bonding and ion-pairing formation [35]. The plot of E onset vs pK a (Supporting Information – Section 3.2) demonstrates that their redox activity and acidity follow the same trend, EHC@tartaric > EHC@citric > EHC@malic, where EHC@tartaric reveals to be the most reactive (highest ability to oxidize) and the most acidic nanomaterial.

Coming to this point, a proposal is given to rationalize the mechanism responsible for the formation of the semiquinone-like nanomaterials. It is proposed the concomitant formation of strong reductants in the anodic compartment during the galvanostatic assay. Such reductants are probably produced by the oxidation of carboxylic acids that possess structural similarities, specifically a α-C(OH) group. It is known from the literature data that a strong reductant species, in the form of a short-lived free radical, can be formed from citric acid by a hydrogen abstraction mechanism. However, to date, such species have been only detected under pulse radiolytic experiments [36,37]. Thus, we postulate that under the present galvanostatic conditions the oxidation of carboxylic acids containing a α-C(OH) group can also occur giving rise to the oxidized radicals, namely, Cit(-H)•, Tar(-H)• and Mal(-H)•, with ability to reduce the oxidized electrogenerated nanomaterial that is released from the graphite anode (probably in the form of a quinone-like species), resulting in a semiquinone-like nanomaterial.

There is significant controversy regarding the exact position where the H-abstraction may occur in the carboxylic acids with α-C(OH) group. It may occur on the hydrogen-bonded to the carbon in α position (at >CH– in tartaric acid or at –CH2– in citric acid) similarly to the oxidation mechanism in primary and secondary alcohols [36,38] or on the hydroxyl group. H-abstraction from the hydroxyl group is suggested by the analysis of the citric acid oxidation products and density functional theory calculations [37,39]. A better clarification of this issue requires the identification of the carboxylic acid electrooxidation products formed by the galvanostatic experimental conditions used in this study, which is out of the scope of this paper. Independently of the hydrogen position, the abovementioned studies consider that H-abstraction takes place by the scavenging of photo-induced OH• radicals. However, the formation of free reactive oxygen species in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is not plausible under the electrolysis conditions used in the present work. Instead, the involvement of adsorbed intermediates like OHads, Oads, and OOHads (represented by the acronym ROSads, Reactive Oxygen Species adsorbed) is more likely on graphite, similarly to what has been proposed for the OER on other electrode materials [40]. Within this framework, it is proposed that H-abstraction from the carboxylic acid compounds results from Eq. (1) (illustrated for the citric acid):
(1)
Cit
. acid
+ RO
S ads
→ Cit
(
− H
)
•
+
H 2
O

However, it is not ruled out the hypothesis that H-abstraction may also occur due to the enormous electric field established between the anode and cathode electrodes [41]. In sum, it is proposed that semiquinone-like nanomaterials are formed throughout the reduction of the quinone-like EHC nanomaterials released from the graphite anode (represented as EHCQ) by the above-mentioned oxidized carboxylic radical, as follows (illustrated for the citric acid):
(2)
EH
C Q
+ Cit
(
− H
)
•
→ EH
C SQ
•
+ Cit
. oxid
. products

Altogether our results suggest that during the galvanostatic polarization the carboxylic acid/carboxylates do not much interfere with the mechanism that is responsible for the CC cleavage on the graphite anode and consequent formation of the highly oxidized hydrophilic carbon nanomaterial. The role of the organic electrolyte seems to be mainly played after the EHC nanomaterial has been generated (in the quinone-like form, as recently demonstrated in a phosphate buffer [25]), following one of two pathways: adsorbing on the EHC surface (the organic electrolyte itself or its oxidation products) or providing the reduction of the EHC nanomaterial (via radical mediation). The adsorption process may completely hinder the surface oxygen functional groups on the EHC nanomaterial, as it was found with EHC@malonic and EHC@succinic, or it may decrease the rate of the electron transfer, as observed with EHC@maleic. It is not ruled out the hypothesis that carboxylic acids may interfere with the water structure in the double layer, making it easier or harder the generation of hydroxyl and oxygen radicals that act as electrochemical “scissors” of carbon-carbon bonds of the graphitic surface during the electrochemical generation of the EHC nanomaterial [42].

Behind the formation of semiquinone-like nanomaterials, another electroactive species was formed throughout the galvanostatic assay or immediately after its ending, as supported by the voltammetric profiles at rather negative potentials ( Fig. 6 ). It is expected that this new species is hydrogen peroxide formed throughout the reduction of molecular oxygen by EHCSQ •, Eq. (3). The thermodynamic feasibility of Eq. (3) was demonstrated in the Supporting Information (Section 3.3) and the formation of hydrogen peroxide was confirmed by peroxide test strips.
(3)
2 EH
C SQ
•
+
O 2
+ 2
H
+
→ 2 EH
C Q
+
H 2
O 2

Eq. (3) is also consistent with the peak current decrease depicted in EHC@tartaric and EHC@malic solutions in the post-synthesis period ( Fig. 8 ). However, it does not explain the unexpected increase in the magnitude of the current oxidation in EHC@citric solutions, neither the current dependence on the citric buffer concentration during the same period. Such results are suggestive that Cit(-H)•, and consequently EHCSQ • (from eq. (2)), is still formed after the switch-off of the galvanostatic cell. This hypothesis is only plausible if an oxidant with the ability to oxidize the citric acid to the radical Cit(-H)• is also present. H2O2 may fulfill this requirement (eq. (4)) but is not precluded the hypothesis that O2 •− may also be formed upon the O2 reduction by EHCSQ • (eq. (5)), contributing to the Cit(-H)• formation, issued from the citric acid oxidation (eq. (6)):
(4)
2 Cit +
H 2
O 2
+ 2
H
+
→ 2 Cit
(
− H
)
•
+ 2
H 2
O
(5)
EH
C SQ
•
+
O 2
→ EH
C Q
+
O 2
• −
(6)
Cit +
O 2
• −
+ 2
H
+
→ Cit
(
− H
)
•
+
H 2
O 2

Considering the standard reduction potentials (vs NHE) of these redox pairs, namely E0
H2O2/H2O = + 1.78 V, E0
Cit(-H)/Cit ≅ + 0.40 V [37], E0
´ EHCSQ• /EHCQ ≅ + 0.233 V (Supporting Information – Section 3.3), E0
O2/O2•- = - 0.18 V, E0
O2•-/H2O2 = + 0.497 V), it can be concluded that Eq. (4), 5 and 6 are thermodynamically feasible.

The intense yellow color of the EHC@citric solution, which prevails after the galvanostatic assay, also supports the O2 •- formation [43]. In contrast, tartaric acid is, apparently, unable to be spontaneously oxidized to Tar(-H)• by the oxidants contained in the EHC@tartaric solution. It is possible that H2O2 is the only oxidant formed upon the O2 reduction by the highly reactive semiquinone-like nanomaterial generated in the tartaric buffer, which is consistent with the colorless of this solution.

The decrease of the current oxidation in EHC@malic solution in a short post-electrolysis period (6 h), seems to be also indicative that the reactions responsible for the Mal(-H)• formation does not occur under no external driving force. However, the voltammetric results depicted with EHC@malic solution along 10 months exclude such possibility. The oxidation of malic acid to the radical Mal(-H)• by H2O2 or O2 •− is foreseen, but at a much lower rate than those predicted in EHC@citric solution (note that EHC@malic shows a slightly yellow color).

Another feature of interest arises in analyzing the current oxidation in EHC@citric solution, recorded immediately after the galvanostatic assay. It is shown that the current oxidation is independent of the citric acid concentration within the 0.044–0.178 M range. A plausible explanation of these results may rely on the assumption that the rate-determining step on the oxidation of citric acid is its adsorption on the graphite electrode. In this way, the rate of Cit(-H)• formation during the galvanostatic polarization could be limited by the surface electrode, rather than by the citric acid concentration in the bulk solution.

In summary, the proposal reactions responsible for the chemical reactions that occur during the galvanostatic assay and in the solution bulk during the post-galvanostatic period in the citric and tartaric buffer solutions are outlined in Fig. 10
.

Despite the mechanistic proposal for the reactions that occur in malic buffer solution are equivalent to those in the citric buffer, the reaction rate responsible for Mal(-H)• formation after the terminus of the galvanostatic essay must be rather lower than for Cit(-H)• generation.

A tentative evaluation of the stability of the EHCSQ • nanomaterial was carried out based on the temporal evolution of its oxidation current, as a reflection of the EHCSQ • concentration profile. However, some prudence must be taken into account in such analysis. As discussed above, a change in its concentration may be the result of two opposite effects: its ability to react with molecular oxygen and to be chemically generated by Cit(-H)•, Tar(-H)• or Mal(-H)• under no external driving force. Over a very short period, corresponding to the timescale of twelve successive voltammetric scans at 10 mV s−1 (≅ 40 min), all the generated solutions display the same jp/jp0 profile, indicative that the three different EHCSQ • radicals show the same stability. However, for longer storing periods (100 min to 15 days), the low stability of the EHC@tartaric solution is demarcated, reflecting its higher electron-donating ability, and consequently its higher facility to react with the available oxygen in an aqueous solution. Interestingly, storing the EHC@tartaric solution at −18 ºC for 15 days leads to much more reactive EHCSQ • nanomaterials than at any other studied storing conditions, as demonstrated by the ≅ 200 mV cathodic shift of the peak potential. We do not have a clear understanding of this behavior but we tentatively interpret it as an increase of the electron density in the aromatic moiety of the semiquinone-like nanomaterial due to supramolecular structures established between dicarboxylic acids/carboxylates electrolytes and the EHCSQ • nanomaterial (e.g. multiple hydrogen-bonding and ion-pairing) at long/low temperature storing conditions. This putative analysis is suggestive that once the EHCSQ • nanomaterial is generated, its electroactivity can be further modulated by an appropriate choice of the electrolyte solution where the nanomaterial is preserved at −18 ºC.

Summarizing, it was demonstrated that the redox properties of water-soluble carbon-based nanomaterials can be tuned by the type of the carboxylic acid buffer (biocompatible buffers) used in the nanomaterial electrochemical generation, opening new perspectives to tailor the electroactivity of these type of nanomaterials for a specific envisioned application.

For the first time, highly water-soluble carbon-based nanomaterials with very strong electron-donating properties were generated in a highly reproducible and rationalized way. This unforeseen electroactivity led us to hypothesize that they are generated in the form of a free radical. EPR and pka data, as well as the number of electrons transferred in their electrooxidation, corroborated our hypothesis, pointing out for a semiquinone-like form.

Unpredictable results were observed when the stability of the semiquinone-like nanomaterials was studied, showing for some of them a progressive activity increase after the terminus of the synthesis. This behavior ushered us to postulate that under no external driving force, the electrolyte itself and a strong oxidant that coexists in solution (most likely, hydrogen peroxide), participate in the reaction responsible for the semiquinone-like nanomaterials formation.

The semiquinone-like carbon-based nanomaterials exhibit a set of properties such as, electron-donating in a non-proton coupled reaction at physiological pH, high ability to be chemically regenerated (in citric or malic acid buffers), and high capacity to diffuse in aqueous medium, that make them great candidates to act as important electron shuttles in living systems to regulate cellular processes in physiological events and prevent/mitigate pathologies associated with electron shuttles dysregulation.

Despite this study shows the key for the rational synthesis of a new emerging therapeutic group of nanomaterials, differences found between the different prepared semiquinone-like nanomaterials call for a detailed investigation on their molecular structure to gather a deepened understanding onto the structure-stability and structure-electrochemical activity relationship.

A.D. Veloso: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. R.A. Videira: Conceptualization, Supervision. M.C. Oliveira: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank FCT (Portugal's Foundation for Science and Technology) for financial support through the CQ-VR (UIDB/00616/2020, UIDP/00616/2020) and LAQV/REQUIMTE (UIDB/50006/2020) Research Units. Andreia D. Veloso thanks FCT, MCTES, ESF, and EU through the individual research pH. D for the Ph.D. scholarship (SFRH/BD/138425/2018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.139734.
